title: Selecting Columns with select()
description: Choose, rename, or drop columns using select() and helper functions.

slides.title: select(): Keep or Drop Columns
slides.content:
`select()` picks columns by name. It's much more flexible than base R's `[]`.

```r
mtcars %>% select(mpg, cyl, hp)
```

You can also drop columns with `-`:

```r
mtcars %>% select(-mpg, -cyl)
```

Use ranges: `select(mpg:hp)` (all columns from mpg to hp).

slides.title: select() Basics
slides.codeSnippet:
library(dplyr)
mtcars %>% select(mpg, cyl, hp)
mtcars %>% select(-disp, -wt)
mtcars %>% select(cyl:gear)

quizData.question: How would you select all columns except 'hp' and 'wt'?

slides.title: Helper Functions in select()
slides.content:
dplyr provides helpers:
- `starts_with("prefix")`
- `ends_with("suffix")`
- `contains("string")`
- `matches("regex")`
- `everything()` – all columns (useful for reordering)
- `last_col()`

Examples:
```r
mtcars %>% select(starts_with("c"))  # columns starting with c (cyl, carb)
mtcars %>% select(ends_with("p"))    # mpg, hp, etc.
mtcars %>% select(mpg, everything())  # bring mpg to front
```

slides.title: Helper Functions
slides.codeSnippet:
library(dplyr)
mtcars %>% select(starts_with("c"))
mtcars %>% select(contains("a"))
mtcars %>% select(mpg, everything())

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: `rename()` is a variant of `select()` that keeps all columns but renames some: `rename(miles_per_gallon = mpg)`.
